chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Have you ever wondered what your phone and house are made of? Looking around, you’ll notice dozens of materials that constitute your reality. What would your life look like if one day they disappeared?

Modern society is so focused on financial freedom and spiritual progress that we forget that, ultimately, this world is material. It's made of stuff without which we would have nothing to live in, create with, or eat from. Without metal, paper, and glass, we would have no entertainment, education, or work. Without textiles, we would still wear fur or freeze because a leaf is not a winter coat.

For centuries, such materials prolonged our survival and improved our lives. Eras of human progress took the name of discovered materials like the Bronze or Iron Age. Our ancestors treated them like gods with awe and gratitude. Now, instead of clay huts, we have skyscrapers; instead of blank spaces, we have ceramic teeth; instead of playing with sticks, we can scroll a phone screen.

Recycle old metal items to reduce waste and save resources.

Follow through to discover the power and fascinating world of the materials surrounding us and how much our lives depend on it.

chapters.title: Steel on trial
chapters.text:
People have used metal for years but only understood its nature in the 20th century. Metals are made of obscure crystals, which makes them non-transparent. Their natural defects or atomic disruptions allow the metal to change shape. For example, billions of such dislocations within a paper clip enable it to bend. Without such imperfections, the metal would snap. A razor contains billions of crystals arranged in a particular order, and when used, it becomes blunt because hairs destroy these crystals.

There’s more. Metalwork started when our ancestors discovered a greenish rock called malachite that turns into copper if heated. This discovery gave birth to the development and growth of civilizations, like the Ancient Egyptians, who used copper chisels to hand-carve stone blocks for the pyramids.

At the same time, they discovered gold. Since it is soft and easily scratched, most accessories are an alloy or a combination of gold, silver, or copper — yep, the gold in your ring is never pure. Due to its scarcity, gold remains one of the most expensive metals on earth.

Eventually, steel, an alloy of iron and carbon, revolutionized the production of tools because it was cheap and robust. However, the proper proportions of elements puzzled most steel makers, so they had to learn and work through trial and error.

Civilizations that made high-quality steel thrived. Japan, where making a samurai's sword became a spiritual ritual, was the most potent steel-making country. Their sword had two parts:

• The central part was made of low-carbon steel — tough and unlikely to break.
• The edge was covered with high-carbon steel — brittle but very sharp.

The Japanese remained the best steel-makers until the Industrial Revolution and the production of ships, bridges, and railways. Then, Henry Bessemer introduced his method, which led to the mass production of steel. Now, it penetrates all spheres of life, from machine building to household necessities. In 1903, King Camp Gillette used the material to sell disposable razors, transforming shaving and creating his business empire.

Clean and store metal items properly to extend their lifespan.

The progress led to the creation of stainless steel that didn't require protective layers of paint. Harry Brearley accidentally discovered that the right proportions of carbon and chromium made a metal that didn't rust. It had an invisible protective layer that, even after scratching, reformed itself. That's how we got cutlery, kitchen sinks, and major appliances.

chapters.title: A clean sheet for paper
chapters.text:
Although we like our books, newspapers, and posters, we rarely understand how much effort it takes to create them. Most paper comes from trees containing two components: cellulose fibers and organic glue called lignin. To make paper, we must complete the following steps:

1. Chop wood into small pieces
2. Boil and chemically process it to separate fibers from lignin, which is just as troublesome as extracting gum from hair
3. Create a watery substance called wood pulp with cellulose floating in it like noodles
4. Dry fibers
5. Make brown paper and give it a glow-up to be accepted into offices, private rooms, and bookstores.

When it comes to the future of paper, the world has separated. The most ruthless war about paper's future involves booklovers. Kindle supporters claim electronic paper perfectly resembles the page's structure without cutting a tree. It's a new sacrifice-free era for paper. However, some adore the smell and touch of the paper and vote for an authentic reading experience. You can even find a funny video of a Le Trefle toilet paper commercial on YouTube called "Emma," where you'll see how intimate and necessary paper can be for reading.

Many cannot imagine life without paper because it gave us many objects we hold dear, including:

• Photos: They represent personal identification and secure your memoirs. Without photo paper, you would not remember your earliest childhood moments.
• Wrapping paper: Perhaps this is the only thing that feels great to destroy. For many, those short moments of seeing a present under the Christmas tree and ruthlessly tearing the paper are the happiest childhood memories.
• Banknotes: Unlike everything else, money is made of cotton, not wood. That's why water doesn't destroy their fibers, and you can wash your jeans without fearing that a hundred in your pocket will be ruined. These are the most seductive, sophisticated, and desired objects worldwide.
• Love letters: You can touch an item your loved one touched, smell it, and put it under your pillow. If your fairytale turns into a pumpkin, you can set the memento on fire.

Choose products with eco-friendly packaging to reduce waste.

Did you know? Instead of cutting trees, the Ukrainian Releaf Paper company processes fallen leaves gathered in urban areas into sustainable paper products like bags and cardboard, reducing CO2 emissions by 78%. Its clients include L'oreal, Schneider Electric, Samsung, and NYX.

chapters.title: No plastic, no equality
chapters.text:
What connects billiard balls and modern cinema? Plastic. To understand this truth, imagine living in the late nineteenth century. You have a job but cannot afford a comb or toothbrush because all are made of ivory, which costs a fortune. However, change came with John Wesley Hyatt's discovery. Encouraged by an advertisement that promised ten thousand dollars for inventing a substitute material for ivory billiard balls, he discovered a material that made VIP trinkets available for the masses — nitrocellulose.

Although Alexander Parkes is officially the first creator of plastic called Parkesine, Hyatt made it into what it is now — a moldable and highly affordable material. Financially supported by General Lefferts, who also invested in young Thomas Edison, Hyatt patented and built a successful company that manufactures celluloid plastic.

Later, Hyatt was sued for stealing intellectual property. Daniel Spill claimed he had created a similar material called Xylonite just a year before. The judge decided that none of the inventors could claim patent rights to the invention, which invited competition and innovations to the industry.

In the 1870s, the celluloid business conquered the world. Ebony and ivory gave way to early plastic materials, just as exclusivity yielded to mass production and equality in consumerism.

Then, nitrocellulose transformed photography. Since glass photographic plates were heavy and expensive, few people could afford a holiday family picture. That's why George Eastman developed celluloid film and invented lightweight, affordable, and compact Kodak cameras, which allowed many to make and keep memories on a plastic scroll.

. . . materials are a reflection of who we are, a multiscale expression of our human needs and desires. ~ Mark Miodownik, PhD

This invention led to yet another innovation — motion pictures. A flexible transparent film was perfect for the role. By quickly rolling it and changing the sequence of photos, the picture came to life. Realizing they could project an image for a bigger audience, the Lumiere brothers established the first cinema.

Purchase biodegradable plastic products to reduce environmental impact.

Eventually, celluloid introduced other forms of plastic:

• Bakelite is an essential material for TVs, radios, and telephones.
• Nylon replaced silk in women's stockings and created new fabrics.
• Vinyl gave the music a clear and authentic sound. Some would say those who didn't listen to vinyl records didn't hear real music.
• Silicone was accidentally advertised by Pamela Anderson in the 1990s and enhanced plastic surgery.

chapters.title: Discovering new worlds through glass
chapters.text:
Books and travel can take you to another culture, either mentally or physically. Yet, if you take a telescope, you'll open not just a new civilization but a whole planet. The same goes for a microscope. This instrument has shown us the structure of every living creature and inanimate object. It promoted genetic engineering and gave women a chance to become mothers through artificial insemination. It all would be impossible without glass.

This material comes from silicon dioxide. Its molecules form quartz crystals found in sand. Their arrangement is similar to water molecules, but their behavior changes under temperature.

When heating both elements, their molecules activate until they reach the point where they can break the bonds that keep them together. It's their melting point. When the water cools down enough to become ice, its molecules rearrange in their previous positions, like soldiers. However, quartz molecules behave like kindergarten children who run in all directions, and when it's time to return to their positions, they have no idea how to do it. So, when the liquid cools down and molecules lose energy, they form a chaotic structure, turning into glass.

The Roman Empire was the most advanced in glass production. It invented glass windows and made mirrors more affordable and sustainable. Moreover, the Romans noticed that molding glass into shape was easier when it was hot enough. By inventing glass-blowing techniques, it manufactured delicate objects like wine glass.

Although Eastern civilizations had much more sophisticated technology and reigned over materials like steel, paper, and ceramics, they ignored glass. This gave the West the upper hand in the scientific revolution. After all, everything, from telescopes to test tubes, is made of glass.

Nowadays, glass is a crucial element of housing, transport, and science. In the 1960s, automobile industries strived to make car windows safer, as in a car crash, the windshield broke into pieces, traumatizing or killing the passengers. Toughened glass appeared after discovering that melted glass's extreme and quick cooling compressed and strengthened it.

Glass became the foundation for all scientific discoveries.

Laminated glass was the new generation of toughened glass that, after breaking, stuck together. It gave rise to bulletproof windows that prevent even an AK-47 rifle from killing you. The trick behind it is layers of plastic and glass forming a cake-like structure, which blunts the bullet and prevents it from penetrating.

chapters.title: What is a lover's drink?
chapters.text:
What would you choose between kissing and eating chocolate? In a 2007 study, Dr. David Lewis conducted an experiment that monitored passionate couples' heart and brain activity when they kissed and ate chocolate. Although kissing provokes a higher heartbeat, chocolate causes more drastic brain activity and a more lasting effect on both regions.

Indeed, most people cannot imagine their lives without chocolate, but few know about the long and unromantic path from cocoa beans to Snickers or Milka bars. Cocoa pods grow on the trunks of coconut trees. Each pod contains thirty to forty soft, almond-shaped seeds called cocoa nuts. People harvest them with machetes and leave them to rot on the ground.

Tropical heat promotes fermentation and decomposition, changing cocoa’s taste. Without this stage, these nuts can't turn into chocolate. However, everything from the weather and the pods' ripeness to the heights of rotting piles affects their taste, making it an extremely complex process.

The material world is not just a display of our technology and culture, it is part of us. ~ Mark Miodownik, PhD

The Mesoamericans, ancient civilizations living in the middle latitudes of the Americas, created a ceremonial drink called chocolatl. They made the first predecessor of hot chocolate drinks by roasting fermented cocoa, adding hot water, and reserving it as an aphrodisiac — a liquid that promotes sexual desire.

European explorers brought the drink to their homeland, but the public didn't favor it. Chocolate was bitter and oily because 50% of a cocoa bean is fat. Nonetheless, in 1828, the Dutch chocolate company Van Houten learned to separate cocoa powder from fat, creating a rich and velvety liquid. Now, the public could appreciate the taste of chocolate.

The new approach of separating cocoa fat from powder led to chocolate bars. The English company Fry and Sons combined the two elements, creating a solid chocolate that melted in the mouth. The cocoa powder's antioxidants provided the product's long shelf life, while added sugar ensured a sweeter and lighter chocolate experience. Then, the Swiss added milk powder produced by Nestle, turning it into a top-rated commercial product.

Substitute sugary desserts with a piece of dark chocolate.

Nowadays, chocolate looks and tastes different from years ago, with additives providing an array of fillings and flavors. Despite adverse nutritious effects, chocolate remains one of the most notable culinary elements, and its consumption is exceptionally high in Northern European countries.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Whatever high spiritual thoughts or intellectual breakthroughs we strive for, nothing would be possible without the tangible things surrounding us. If our ancestors hadn't learned to make paper, we wouldn't read and write books. If they hadn't invented toughened glass, our offices would have less light and comfort. And if they didn't invent chocolate… well, life would be less enjoyable.
Every object and tool you use encapsulates and reflects a long and passionate human experience. Respecting and appreciating it is our duty. So, before you get mad at that old car you drive or judge people who read printed newspapers, take a minute to appreciate what it gave us: comfort, freedom, and knowledge.

Try this

• Cultivate mindfulness when buying and throwing things away.
• Think about materials that make your life richer and in what way. Learn to appreciate it.
• Watch The Future of Materials lecture by Mark Miodownik on Youtube.
